---
title: "Spectral Inequalities and Variational Principles"
description: "How symmetric matrices turn quadratic-form reasoning into eigenvalue bounds, and why Rayleigh quotients and min-max principles are the cleanest first-pass spectral tools."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: matrix-analysis
prerequisites:
  - /topics/advanced/matrix-analysis/concepts/positive-semidefinite-matrices-and-quadratic-forms.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Understand why symmetric matrices are special for spectral reasoning."
  - "Define the Rayleigh quotient and interpret it as directionwise energy per unit length."
  - "Use first-pass variational principles to identify extreme eigenvalues."
  - "Recognize the main spectral inequalities that follow from quadratic-form and PSD-order reasoning."
applications:
  - optimization
  - random-matrices
  - machine-learning-theory
keywords:
  - spectral theorem
  - Rayleigh quotient
  - variational principle
  - eigenvalue bounds
  - PSD order
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/advanced/matrix-analysis/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/matrix-analysis/concepts/positive-semidefinite-matrices-and-quadratic-forms.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/high-dimensional-probability/concepts/random-matrices-and-spectral-concentration.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "MIT 18.06 Linear Algebra"
  - "Stanford EE364a"
  - "Boyd and Vandenberghe, Convex Optimization"
current-sources:
  - "MIT 18.06 lecture notes"
  - "Cornell CS6241 symmetric eigenvalue notes"
  - "Cornell CS6210 matrix computations notes"
  - "Stanford EE364a course page"
---

## Role

This is the third page of the `Matrix Analysis` module.

The PSD page said that symmetric matrices can be tested through quadratic forms.

This page shows how that same quadratic-form viewpoint turns directly into eigenvalue bounds.

That is the core spectral move:

`turn a matrix question into a max/min problem over directions`

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after `Positive Semidefinite Matrices and Quadratic Forms`.

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- why symmetric matrices admit especially clean spectral reasoning
- what the Rayleigh quotient measures
- how extreme eigenvalues arise from max/min principles
- why many useful spectral inequalities are really quadratic-form inequalities in disguise

## Why It Matters

Many theorems do not ask for every eigenvalue exactly.

They ask for bounds like:

- how large can the top eigenvalue be?
- how small can the bottom eigenvalue be?
- how does a matrix compare to another one in every direction?
- what is the worst curvature, variance, or amplification?

Variational principles answer these questions without requiring a full eigendecomposition every time.

This is why they show up constantly in:

- optimization through Hessian bounds
- high-dimensional probability through random-matrix concentration
- kernel and covariance methods through Gram matrices
- graph and spectral ML through Laplacians and energy forms

## Prerequisite Recall

- symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues
- PSD structure is a statement about quadratic forms
- operator norms measure worst-case amplification
- quadratic forms already let us test a matrix by probing directions

## Intuition

### The Rayleigh Quotient

For a symmetric matrix $A$, the quantity

$$
\frac{x^\top A x}{x^\top x}
$$

measures the energy of the matrix in the direction $x$, normalized by the size of $x$.

If $x$ happens to be an eigenvector, this quotient is exactly the eigenvalue.

So the Rayleigh quotient is the right bridge between:

- arbitrary directions
- actual eigen-directions

### Why Max And Min Matter

If you sweep over all nonzero directions, the largest Rayleigh quotient gives the top eigenvalue and the smallest gives the bottom one.

That turns spectral questions into optimization problems over the sphere.

### Spectral Inequalities As Directionwise Bounds

Many matrix inequalities become transparent once you say:

`for every vector x, compare x^T A x and x^T B x`

If one quadratic form is always smaller than another, then the corresponding eigenvalues and operator-level quantities are often ordered too.

## Formal Core

::: {#thm-spectral-theorem}
## Theorem Idea: Spectral Theorem For Symmetric Matrices

If $A$ is a real symmetric matrix, then there exists an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors and a decomposition

$$
A = Q \Lambda Q^\top,
$$

where $Q$ is orthogonal and $\Lambda$ is diagonal with real eigenvalues.
:::

This is what makes symmetric matrices especially friendly: geometry and spectrum line up cleanly.

::: {#def-rayleigh}
## Definition: Rayleigh Quotient

For a symmetric matrix $A$ and nonzero vector $x$, the Rayleigh quotient is

$$
R_A(x)=\frac{x^\top A x}{x^\top x}.
$$
:::

If $x$ is a unit vector, this simplifies to

$$
R_A(x)=x^\top A x.
$$

::: {#thm-extreme-variational}
## Theorem Idea: Extreme Eigenvalues As Variational Quantities

For a symmetric matrix $A$,

$$
\lambda_{\max}(A)=\max_{\|x\|_2=1} x^\top A x,
\qquad
\lambda_{\min}(A)=\min_{\|x\|_2=1} x^\top A x.
$$
:::

So the top and bottom eigenvalues are optimization problems over unit directions.

::: {#thm-rayleigh-bounds}
## Theorem Idea: Every Rayleigh Quotient Lies Between The Extreme Eigenvalues

For every nonzero vector $x$,

$$
\lambda_{\min}(A)
\le
\frac{x^\top A x}{x^\top x}
\le
\lambda_{\max}(A).
$$
:::

This is one of the most useful first-pass spectral inequalities.

::: {#thm-psd-order-spectral}
## Theorem Idea: PSD Order Gives Spectral Bounds

If $A$ and $B$ are symmetric and

$$
A \preceq B,
$$

meaning

$$
x^\top A x \le x^\top B x
\qquad \text{for all }x,
$$

then

$$
\lambda_{\max}(A)\le \lambda_{\max}(B)
\qquad \text{and} \qquad
\lambda_{\min}(A)\le \lambda_{\min}(B).
$$
:::

The first-pass message is simple:

- compare quadratic forms in every direction
- get spectral comparisons as a consequence

## Worked Example

Consider

$$
A=
\begin{bmatrix}
4 & 0\\
0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

For a unit vector $x=(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$,

$$
x^\top A x
=
4\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta.
$$

This quantity is largest when $\theta=0$, giving

$$
\lambda_{\max}(A)=4,
$$

and smallest when $\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}$, giving

$$
\lambda_{\min}(A)=1.
$$

So here the Rayleigh quotient makes the variational principle completely visible:

- the sphere of directions is the search space
- the eigen-directions are exactly the maximizing and minimizing directions

Now compare with

$$
B=
\begin{bmatrix}
5 & 0\\
0 & 2
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

Since

$$
x^\top A x \le x^\top B x
\qquad \text{for every }x,
$$

we get

$$
\lambda_{\max}(A)\le \lambda_{\max}(B)
$$

and similarly for the bottom eigenvalue.

That is the core pattern behind many spectral inequalities.

## Computation Lens

When a theorem contains an eigenvalue bound, try rewriting it as:

1. a maximization or minimization over unit vectors
2. a quadratic-form comparison
3. a PSD-order comparison

That translation often reveals what the proof is really doing.

## Application Lens

### Optimization

The largest and smallest eigenvalues of a Hessian control smoothness and curvature. Variational principles turn those quantities into directional tests.

### High-Dimensional Probability

Random-matrix concentration often aims to show that

$$
\|A-\mathbb E A\|_{\mathrm{op}}
$$

is small, which for symmetric matrices is directly tied to extreme eigenvalue control.

### Machine Learning

Kernel matrices, covariance operators, and graph Laplacians are often analyzed through Rayleigh quotients and spectral inequalities rather than full diagonalization.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you can now explain:

- what the Rayleigh quotient measures
- why extreme eigenvalues arise from max/min problems over directions
- why quadratic-form comparisons imply spectral inequalities
- why symmetric matrices are much easier to analyze spectrally than arbitrary matrices

then this page has done its job.

## Go Deeper

After this page, the next natural step is:

- [Perturbation and Stability](perturbation-and-stability.qmd)

The strongest adjacent live pages right now are:

- [Positive Semidefinite Matrices and Quadratic Forms](positive-semidefinite-matrices-and-quadratic-forms.qmd)
- [Perturbation and Stability](perturbation-and-stability.qmd)
- [Random Matrices and Spectral Concentration](../../high-dimensional-probability/concepts/random-matrices-and-spectral-concentration.qmd)
- [Optimization](../../optimization/index.qmd)
- [Eigenvalues and Diagonalization](../../../foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/eigenvalues-and-diagonalization.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this page are:

- [MIT 18.06 lecture notes](https://www.mit.edu/~18.06/Fall19/Lecture%20notes.pdf) - official notes with clean positive-(semi)definite and eigenvalue background. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Cornell CS6241 symmetric eigenvalue notes](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/courses/cs6241/2025sp/lec/2025-02-13.html) - official notes connecting symmetric matrices, quadratic forms, and eigenvalue viewpoints. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Cornell CS6210 matrix computations notes](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/courses/cs6210/2025fa/lec/2025-08-25.pdf) - official notes with a compact operator-norm and spectral summary. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE364a: Convex Optimization I](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364a/index.html) - official current course page where spectral bounds and PSD structure feed directly into optimization reasoning. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.06 lecture notes](https://www.mit.edu/~18.06/Fall19/Lecture%20notes.pdf) - `First pass` - official notes for the cleanest first-pass route through quadratic forms and eigenvalue bounds. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Cornell CS6241 symmetric eigenvalue notes](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/courses/cs6241/2025sp/lec/2025-02-13.html) - `First pass` - official notes highlighting the quadratic-form and symmetric-eigenvalue viewpoint. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Cornell CS6210 matrix computations notes](https://www.cs.cornell.edu/courses/cs6210/2025fa/lec/2025-08-25.pdf) - `Second pass` - official notes with a stronger matrix-computations perspective on norms and spectra. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford EE364a: Convex Optimization I](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee364a/index.html) - `Second pass` - official optimization course page where spectral inequalities keep reappearing through Hessian and PSD arguments. Checked `2026-04-25`.
